name: notable blue ring amulet
shortDescription: A notable blue ring amulet shaped as a small loop of bright charm-metal around a polished blue center. Sailors, couriers, and anxious heirs favor it for its clean color and tidy silhouette, claiming it keeps promises from slipping loose.
flavorText: “Blue charms are bought for calm and worn for courage.”

name: green ring amulet
shortDescription: A green ring amulet with a simple circular frame and moss-colored stone, commonly sold as a token of growth, safe travel, and steady fortune. Herbalists like its color, farmers like its symbolism, and merchants like how easily both groups haggle for it.
flavorText: “A green charm promises that something will take root.”

name: red ring amulet
shortDescription: A red ring amulet made to catch the eye with a warm, insistent glow. It is bought for passion, resolve, warnings, and dramatic gifts, though practical traders mostly respect that red jewelry is very hard for customers to ignore.
flavorText: “Red charms rarely whisper.”

name: market cyan sphere amulet
shortDescription: A market cyan sphere amulet set with a rounded bead the color of clear shallows. It is common enough for stalls but bright enough to feel special, making it a favorite purchase for travelers who want a little luck without paying temple prices.
flavorText: “Cyan glass sells the dream of open water in a crowded street.”

name: green sphere amulet
shortDescription: A green sphere amulet with a smooth rounded charm that rolls light across its surface like a tiny forest moon. Buyers tie it to harvest luck, healing hopes, and gentle protection, which gives sellers plenty of polite stories to choose from.
flavorText: “Round green stones make worry feel a little less sharp.”

name: pink sphere amulet
shortDescription: A pink sphere amulet with a soft polished charm, popular for courtship gifts, nursery blessings, and sentimental keepsakes. It is not the strongest-looking talisman on a stall, but it has a way of making buyers lower their voices.
flavorText: “Gentle charms are purchased with the least bargaining.”

name: blue stone amulet
shortDescription: A blue stone amulet set in a modest frame, cool to the eye and easy to pair with travel cloaks or scholar robes. It carries a reputation for patience and clear thought, especially among buyers who need more of both.
flavorText: “The stone does not make decisions better, but it gives doubt something pretty to hold.”

name: green stone amulet
shortDescription: A green stone amulet with an earthy face and practical setting, often sold to gardeners, healers, and caravan folk who like charms that feel grounded. Its value rises when the stone shows clean color without cracks or cloudy veins.
flavorText: “A good green stone looks like a field after rain.”

name: red stone amulet
shortDescription: A red stone amulet that carries warmth, urgency, and a little theatrical danger in its polished face. Duelists, lovers, merchants, and performers all find different reasons to want one, which makes it easy stock for a clever jeweler.
flavorText: “Red stones sell best to people already convinced they are bold.”

name: blue triangle amulet
shortDescription: A blue triangle amulet cut into sharp, deliberate geometry, prized by navigators, students, and ritualists who believe shape can discipline luck. It hangs neatly, points cleanly, and makes ordinary robes look as if the wearer has a plan.
flavorText: “Three corners, one calm lie: everything is under control.”

name: green triangle amulet
shortDescription: A green triangle amulet with a crisp frame and leafy color, often associated with growth directed by purpose. Buyers favor it for apprenticeships, planting seasons, new shops, and other ventures that need both luck and a nudge toward order.
flavorText: “Growth behaves better when given corners.”

name: magenta triangle amulet
shortDescription: A magenta triangle amulet bright enough to look intentional from across a crowded market. It is favored by performers, young mages, and fashionable buyers who want a charm that feels unusual without requiring a long explanation from the seller.
flavorText: “Some colors do not match clothes; they start arguments with them.”

name: market amulet of bats
shortDescription: A market amulet of bats, stamped or carved with small winged shapes for night luck, cave roads, and quiet exits. Miners, scouts, and theatrical thieves all claim different traditions for it, though most are buying the same useful darkness.
flavorText: “Bats know every ceiling has another path.”

name: scarce amulet of flying
shortDescription: A scarce amulet of flying, light in the hand and heavily burdened with rumor. Most copies are symbolic rather than miraculous, but couriers, cliff towns, and ambitious nobles still pay well for anything that suggests the ground is negotiable.
flavorText: “Whether it lifts the body or only the price depends on the seller.”

name: notable Gravebinding bloodlust necklace
shortDescription: A notable Gravebinding bloodlust necklace with grim ornament and ritual weight, traded quietly among collectors of dangerous-looking relics. Its name alone raises prices in shadowed shops, though serious buyers inspect the clasp before they believe the story.
flavorText: “Some necklaces are worn. This one is endured.”

name: notable Gravebinding bloodlust neckring
shortDescription: A rigid Gravebinding bloodlust neckring, notable for its severe shape and unsettling ceremonial style. It attracts war-priests, curse scholars, and nobles with dreadful taste, all of whom pretend they are purchasing history rather than intimidation.
flavorText: “A neckring leaves no doubt that comfort was not the point.”

name: fabled amulet of shielding
shortDescription: A fabled amulet of shielding said to turn aside ill fortune, foul glances, and whatever else a frightened buyer can imagine. Whether its power is true or not, guards and travelers pay for the comfort of something between them and the unknown.
flavorText: “A shield worn at the throat protects mostly the heart behind it.”

name: market amulet of healing
shortDescription: A market amulet of healing sold with gentle colors, careful knots, and enough hopeful language to soothe customers before any cure begins. Healers dislike exaggerated claims, but even they admit a worried patient sometimes needs a charm to hold.
flavorText: “Not all remedies are swallowed.”

name: market amulet of anarchy
shortDescription: A market amulet of anarchy marked with crooked lines and rebellious little symbols, popular with students, smugglers, performers, and anyone briefly furious at taxes. Most are harmless trinkets, but they sell briskly wherever order has grown too smug.
flavorText: “The cheapest rebellion is worn on a string.”

name: market necromancer finger necklace
shortDescription: A market necromancer finger necklace, more macabre souvenir than certified relic, though sellers rarely phrase it that plainly. Occult collectors, prankish nobles, and gloomy apprentices examine it with far more seriousness than most stalls deserve.
flavorText: “The dead are expensive when sold in pieces of story.”

name: scarce amulet of luck
shortDescription: A scarce amulet of luck carried by gamblers, sailors, messengers, and merchants who swear they do not believe in such things until the dice are nearly cast. Its value depends less on proof than on how many fortunate owners can be named.
flavorText: “Luck sells best to people who claim they make their own.”

name: scarce amulet of four winds
shortDescription: A scarce amulet of four winds, often marked with crossing lines, feathers, or tiny directional charms. Navigators and caravan masters prize it as a travel token, especially before long roads where maps end and weather begins giving opinions.
flavorText: “Four winds mean four chances to blame the journey.”

name: fabled amulet of vitality
shortDescription: A fabled amulet of vitality with bright fittings and a reputation for strengthening the weary. It is bought by aging nobles, champion runners, sickroom families, and desperate adventurers, all of whom want endurance to be something that can be worn.
flavorText: “The body tires. Hope keeps shopping.”

name: fabled magic lamp
shortDescription: A fabled magic lamp polished to a tempting shine and surrounded by stories too profitable to settle. Collectors want it for wonder, mages want it for study, and merchants mostly want buyers to ask whether anything lives inside.
flavorText: “Never promise wishes unless the lamp has already paid for itself.”

name: crystal of entrophy
shortDescription: A crystal of entrophy with fractured light and a troubling habit of making neat rooms feel temporary. Scholars debate the spelling as often as the power, while cautious traders wrap it separately from anything they hope will remain orderly.
flavorText: “Some crystals sparkle. This one disagrees with structure.”

name: crystal of Reshaping
shortDescription: A crystal of Reshaping said to soften the boundaries between what a thing is and what it might become. Artificers, mages, and reckless inventors pay well for clean facets, especially when the stone seems to bend reflected lines.
flavorText: “Change is easier to sell when it fits in a velvet pouch.”

name: fabled powerful orb
shortDescription: A fabled powerful orb with a deep inner glow and the sort of weight that makes handlers whisper even when nothing happens. It is an expensive object because everyone agrees it could matter terribly under the right moon, ritual, or buyer.
flavorText: “An orb does not need to explain itself; round mysteries rarely do.”

name: fabled mirror of phantoms
shortDescription: A fabled mirror of phantoms, silvered darkly enough that reflections seem to arrive late. The mirror is coveted by occult houses and banned by sensible innkeepers, since guests dislike furniture that looks back with extra company.
flavorText: “Some mirrors show faces. Others collect them.”

name: simple candle
shortDescription: A simple candle of plain wax and honest wick, useful for homes, shrines, kitchens, ledgers, and every dark corner not important enough for a lantern. It is cheap, necessary, and always more valuable after sunset than before.
flavorText: “One small flame can make a room remember its shape.”

name: crude candle
shortDescription: A crude candle with uneven wax, smoky manners, and a wick that may require patience. It is not pleasant, but it is affordable light for cellars, camps, workshops, and anyone who values seeing more than smelling refinement.
flavorText: “Bad candles still beat perfect darkness.”

name: scented candle
shortDescription: A scented candle blended with oils or herbs to make small rooms kinder to the nose. Nobles buy it for comfort, healers for calm, and innkeepers for covering evidence that last night's stew was a mistake.
flavorText: “A good scent can negotiate with a bad room.”

name: fancy candelabra
shortDescription: A fancy candelabra with decorative arms and enough polish to turn ordinary candlelight into a household performance. It is bought for dining halls, parlors, ceremonies, and any room where shadows should look expensive rather than accidental.
flavorText: “Candlelight becomes status when given branches.”

name: fancy candle
shortDescription: A fancy candle molded with color, scent, or ornamental shaping, intended for gifts, rituals, fine tables, and customers who think plain wax lacks ambition. It burns like any candle, but it earns more coin before doing so.
flavorText: “Luxury is sometimes just wax that knows it will disappear.”

name: cooking board
shortDescription: A cooking board scarred for chopping, kneading, slicing, and every kitchen task too practical for ceremony. Cooks judge it by grain, weight, and how well it stays put when a hurried knife begins arguing with supper.
flavorText: “A good board takes the blame for every meal's first wound.”

name: cooking pot
shortDescription: A cooking pot sturdy enough for stews, grains, broths, beans, and whatever can be rescued by heat and salt. It is common stock, but a settlement with pots and fuel is never entirely poor while something can still simmer.
flavorText: “Civilization smells like a pot that has been used all week.”

name: cooking ladle
shortDescription: A cooking ladle with a long handle and deep bowl, made for serving soup, stirring sauces, and keeping hands out of trouble. Taverns buy them in bunches because no kitchen argument has ever improved by sharing one ladle.
flavorText: “The ladle decides who receives the generous bowl.”

name: scarce cloth of augmentation
shortDescription: A scarce cloth of augmentation woven with strange threads that seem to hold light longer than ordinary fabric. Enchanters buy it for experiments, tailors buy it with caution, and merchants fold it carefully away from skeptical fingers.
flavorText: “Some cloth covers the body. Some hints at improvements.”

name: fabled crown of decay
shortDescription: A fabled crown of decay with tarnished grandeur and a legend that makes even collectors avoid holding it too long. It is valuable for exactly the wrong reasons: old power, bad endings, and the irresistible market for cursed-looking splendor.
flavorText: “Rot wears gold when it wants to be remembered.”

name: fabled crown of electricity
shortDescription: A fabled crown of electricity, bright with sharp ornament and stories of storms answering royal thoughts. Mages inspect its points, nobles admire its danger, and porters refuse to carry it in rain without a special fee.
flavorText: “Lightning is more dignified when given a head to circle.”

name: fabled crown of eternal torment
shortDescription: A fabled crown of eternal torment, infamous in grim songs and better locked displays. Its craftsmanship is undeniable and its reputation worse, making it a prize for collectors who confuse dreadful history with excellent taste.
flavorText: “Some crowns prove a throne can be a trap with cushions.”

name: scarce cage of Unraveling
shortDescription: A scarce cage of Unraveling built from thin, unsettling bars that seem arranged to hold more than animals. Scholars argue whether it contains, focuses, or merely symbolizes decay of form, but everyone agrees it should be carried gently.
flavorText: “A cage is most worrying when no one knows what escaped it.”

name: lightning scales
shortDescription: Lightning scales that shimmer like stormlight caught on armor, traded as rare magical material by artificers and charm-smiths. Each scale feels light but valuable, especially to buyers hoping to bottle speed, shock, or dramatic presentation.
flavorText: “Storms shed no skins, which is why these cost so much.”

name: enchantment cornuthaum
shortDescription: An enchantment cornuthaum shaped for ritual wear, lecture halls, and mages who believe a pointed silhouette improves concentration. Its worth depends on stitching, symbols, and whether apprentices lower their voices when it is placed on the table.
flavorText: “A hat can be silly until the runes begin glowing.”

name: Gravebinding hat
shortDescription: A Gravebinding hat with somber cloth, ritual trim, and a style associated with those who study old names and restless ground. It is niche stock, but the right buyer treats it as professional equipment rather than costume.
flavorText: “Some hats shade the eyes. This one shades the conversation.”

name: apprentice mage hat
shortDescription: An apprentice mage hat with modest stitching and more ambition than authority. It is commonly bought for new students, stage conjurers, and children of optimistic families, all of whom discover quickly that cloth is cheaper than tuition.
flavorText: “Every master once owned a hat too large for their confidence.”

name: illusion hat
shortDescription: An illusion hat stitched with misleading folds, bright lining, and a brim that seems to sit slightly wrong when watched too closely. Performers and trick-mages love it, while merchants appreciate that mystery covers minor tailoring flaws.
flavorText: “The best illusion is convincing buyers the hat chose them.”

name: powerful Unraveling hat
shortDescription: A powerful Unraveling hat marked with severe patterns and a reputation for loosening spells, knots, plans, and composure. It is sold with care to expert practitioners, or to wealthy fools at prices high enough to discourage questions.
flavorText: “Wear it only if you trust what remains after things come apart.”

name: Lantern-Summoning hat
shortDescription: A Lantern-Summoning hat decorated with warm sigils and traveler charms, favored by night wardens, tunnel mages, and performers who prefer their entrances well lit. Even dormant, it gives off the hopeful look of a road finding its lamp.
flavorText: “Darkness is less confident around hats with instructions for light.”

name: Reshaping hat
shortDescription: A Reshaping hat with flexible brim, shifting trim, and the uneasy suggestion that its form is still negotiating. Tailor-mages and transformation scholars value it as a focus, while ordinary buyers simply wonder why it never looks the same twice.
flavorText: “A hat of Reshaping never quite agrees with the mirror.”

name: Unraveling hat
shortDescription: An Unraveling hat used by practitioners who study loosened bindings, opened patterns, and the careful undoing of stubborn magic. It is less dramatic than the powerful version, but easier to sell to students still learning which threads not to pull.
flavorText: “Every knot is a lesson until someone pulls the wrong end.”
